title: Cat Rapid Breathing While Resting: Triage and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat breathing fast while sleeping? Learn why rapid resting breathing is a high-priority emergency and how to help your pet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rapid breathing in a resting cat is a high-priority veterinary emergency that indicates the body is struggling to maintain oxygen. A healthy cat should take 16 to 30 breaths per minute while relaxed; anything higher requires immediate medical attention.

What is considered rapid resting breathing in cats?

Rapid breathing while a cat is at rest or sleeping is medically known as tachypnea. While it is normal for a cat to breathe more quickly after a vigorous play session or if they are feeling hot, breathing fast while relaxed is a significant clinical sign. A healthy cat typically takes between 16 to 30 breaths per minute while resting. If your cat is consistently exceeding this rate without physical exertion, it indicates their body is working overtime to maintain oxygen levels.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my cat is breathing fast while resting?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, rapid breathing while resting is considered a veterinary emergency. Unlike dogs, cats rarely pant, so any sign of increased respiratory effort is a major red flag. This symptom can be caused by life-threatening conditions, including:

  • Congestive heart failure or feline asthma
  • Pneumonia or pleural effusion (fluid around the lungs)
  • Open-mouth breathing or stretching the neck out to breathe
  • A bluish tint to the gums

How can a photo or video of my cat help the emergency triage process?

When you contact an emergency vet or a triage service, providing a short video of your cat's breathing can be life-saving. A video provides the veterinary team with critical visual information:

  • It shows specific breathing patterns, such as abdominal effort (the belly moving significantly) or shallow chest movements.
  • It helps the staff prioritize your cat’s care the moment you walk through the door.
  • It allows the team to prepare necessary oxygen therapy or diagnostic equipment in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in cats manifests with an increased respiratory rate and effort, immediately followed by a change in respiratory pattern. Cats may sit crouched on all four limbs with the sternum slightly elevated (orthopnea). Obvious labored, open-mouth breathing and changes in mucous membrane color (gray and/or blue [cyanosis]) develop last and indicate significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. Pleural space disease causes asynchronous breathing; lung parenchymal disease causes quiet, smooth breathing, with the chest and abdominal wall moving in the same direction. Taking radiographs or performing stressful diagnostic procedures before the animal has been stabilized can lead to rapid decompensation.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: How do I count my cat's respiratory rate?
faqs.answer: Count how many times the chest rises and falls in 30 seconds while the cat is resting, then multiply that number by two to get the breaths per minute.

faqs.question: Can stress cause rapid breathing in cats?
faqs.answer: While stress and anxiety can increase a cat's breathing rate, it should return to normal once the stressor is removed. If the rapid breathing persists while the cat is calm or asleep, it is a medical issue.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool and quiet. Minimize handling the cat and keep them in a secure carrier to reduce further stress, which can worsen respiratory distress.
